texts.orig: Carbon dioxide capture and storage (CCS) is essential to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and addresses the global climate change. Absorption/desorption with aqueous amine solutions is the state-of-the-art technology for the post combustion capture (PCC) of CO2 from coal-fired power plants. Compared to other CCS techniques like pre-combustion capture and oxy-fuel technology, PCC is a mature process and can be implemented on a large scale in a relatively short time. A major obstacle hindering the implementation of absorption/desorption processes with aqueous amine solutions on a large scale for PCC is the high capital and especially the high operating cost. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Application of new solvents will substantially contribute to the reduction of the energy demand for the post combustion capture of CO2 from power plant flue gases. The present work describes tests of such new solvents in a gas-fired pilot plant, which comprises the complete absorption/desorption process (column diameters 0.125 m, absorber/desorber packing height 4.25/2.55 m, packing type: Sulzer BX 500, flue gas flow 30-100 kg/h, CO2 partial pressure 35-135 mbar). Two new solvents CESAR1 (0.28 g/g 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol þ 0.17 g/g piperazine þ 0.55 g/g H2O) and CESAR2 (0.32 g/g 1, 2-ethanediamine þ 0.68 g/g H2O), which were developed in an EU-project, were systematically studied and compared to MEA (0.3 g/g monoethanolamine þ 0.7 g/g H2O). The two new solvents and MEA were studied in the same way in the pilot plant and detailed results are reported for all solvents. In the present study the structured packing Sulzer BX 500 is used. The measurements are carried out at a constant CO2 removal rate of 90% by an adjustment of the regeneration energy in the desorber for systematically varied solvent flow rates. An optimal solvent flow rate leading to a minimum energy requirement is found from these studies. Direct comparisons of such results can be misleading if there are differences in the kinetics of the different solvent systems. The influence of kinetic effects is experimentally studied by varying the flue gas flow rate at a constant ratio of solvent mass flow to flue gas mass flow and constant CO2 removal rate. Results from these studies indicate similar kinetics for CESAR1, CESAR2 and MEA. The direct comparison of the pilot plant results for these solvents is therefore justified. Both CESAR1 and CESAR2 show improvements compared to MEA. The most promising is CESAR1 with a reduction of about 20% in the regeneration energy and 45% in the solvent flow rate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To achieve further progress in the development of economic PCC, it is essential to find more efficient new solvents. This was the task of work carried out in the frame of the integrated European Union project CESAR (2011). Within this project solvent candidates were screened and ranked. Selected promising solvents were then tested in pilot plants. The present paper reports on results of such tests.

texts.orig: A gas-fired absorption/desorption pilot plant for removal of carbon dioxide from flue gases was used for the present study. The plant was initially set up and operated at the University of Stuttgart, Germany, and has later been transferred to the University of Kaiserslautern, Germany, where it is presently being operated. The pilot plant has been described in detail by Notz et al. (submitted for publication). Supplementary information is available in Notz (2010).

texts.orig: The flow scheme of the absorption/desorption process for post combustion CO2 capture with aqueous amine solutions is shown in Fig. 1. The main specifications of the pilot plant used in the present study are given in Table 1. The flue gas produced by a natural gas burner is fed into the pre-washer column by a blower. The flue gas flow rate can be set approximately between 30 and 150 kg/h. The maximum gas flow rate through the absorber is limited to approximately 100 kg/h ( F -factor E 2.1 Pa 0.5 ) due to fluid dynamic limitations. The pre-washer is built as a direct contact cooler to set the temperature of the flue gas at the absorber inlet and at the same time to make sure that the flue gas is saturated with water. The flue gas enters the absorber at the bottom with a temperature of approximately 40-50 1 C. The absorber is built of five sections, which are each equipped with 0.85 m (5 packing elements) of the structured packing BX 500 (Sulzer Chemtec, Winterthur, Switzerland). The total packing height is 4.25 m. The lean solvent is fed to the absorber top, typically at a temperature of 40 1 C. Upon the CO2 absorption into the liquid phase, absorption enthalpy is released, which leads to a temperature increase. texts.label: text
texts.orig: For steady state operation the liquid level in the absorber bottom is controlled by a pump. The rich solvent is pumped into the desorber through the rich lean heat exchanger, where the CO2 rich solvent is heated to higher temperatures through the lean solvent from the desorber bottom. The desorber is built of three sections, which are each equipped with five elements of BX 500 similar to the absorber. The total packing height in the desorber is 2.55 m. Both the absorber and desorber columns have a diameter of 0.125 m. The bottom of the desorber contains electrical heating elements for partial evaporation of the solvent. For aqueous amine solutions, mainly water is evaporated. The vapor at the top of the desorber consists of water, CO2 and some traces of amine. To retain the amine, also at the desorber top a washing section is installed. The vapor at the desorber top is led into the condenser where most of the water is removed so that almost pure CO2 is obtained. A part of the condensate is used in the washing section of the desorber and another part if necessary is withdrawn to fulfill the water balance of the absorption/desorption process. For a more detailed description of the pilot plant and its operation, see Notz et al. (submitted for publication).

texts.text: 3. Pilot plant experiments with new solvents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aqueous solutions of amines are the preferred solvents for post combustion CO2 capture. It is especially attractive to combine the advantages of primary, secondary and tertiary amines in blends (Notz et al., 2007). CESAR1 (0.28 g/g 2-amino-2-methyl-1propanol þ 0.17 g/g piperazine þ 0.55 g/g H2O) is a mixture of primary and secondary amine, whereas CESAR2 (0.32 g/g 1, 2ethanediamine þ 0.68 g/g H2O) is a primary amine with two amine groups. Having passed several pre-selection criteria like equilibrium data of the CO2 solubility, mass transfer data, reaction kinetics, volatility, stability and toxicity, they were tested in the pilot plant and compared to MEA.

texts.orig: process can be seen easily. The regeneration energy needed in the desorber can be separated into the following contributions (Mangalapally et al., 2009; Notz et al., 2011): the energy needed for (1) desorption of CO2 (desorption enthalpy), (2) heating up the solvent, (3) heating up the reflux condensate and (4) for generating the stripping stream. The distance between the equilibrium curves at high and low temperature is closely related to the solvent flow rate but also influences the stripping stream demand. A large distance is desired and will lead to lower solvent flow rate but generally also to a lower regeneration energy as the separation task becomes easier (Notz, 2010; Notz et al., 2011).

A large distance is desired and will lead to lower solvent flow rate but generally also to a lower regeneration energy as the separation task becomes easier (Notz, 2010; Notz et al., 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 2 shows that CESAR1 and CESAR2 have larger distances between the equilibrium curves than MEA. Hence, for CESAR1 and CESAR2 lower optimal solvent flow rates should be expected compared to MEA, and there is hope that also the regeneration energies are favorable. Especially CESAR1 looks promising due to the availability of large distance between the equilibrium curves. In the above discussion mass transfer kinetics are not considered. Slow kinetics lead to the need for high columns and/or efficient packings. Furthermore, Fig. 2 also highlights that the equilibrium curves at 40 and 120 1 C for CESAR2 are at much higher CO2 loading numbers as compared to both MEA and CESAR1, respectively. So, it is expected that higher CO2 rich/lean loadings are observed for CESAR2 as compared to MEA and CESAR1, respectively.

texts.orig: 3.2. Methodology for solvent comparison in the pilot plant
texts.text: 3.2. Methodology for solvent comparison in the pilot plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For testing solvents in the pilot plant, a consistent method was defined and applied to all solvents. There are two basic sets of experiments the ''variation of solvent flow rate'' and the ''variation of flue gas flow rate''. They are described in the next paragraphs.
texts.text: For testing solvents in the pilot plant, a consistent method was defined and applied to all solvents. There are two basic sets of experiments the ''variation of solvent flow rate'' and the ''variation of flue gas flow rate''. They are described in the next paragraphs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this set of experiments the solvent flow rate is varied at constant flue gas flow rate. Hence, the ratio of the two mass flow rates, the L / G ratio, is varied. The CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas is fixed. Furthermore, the CO2 removal rate ( C CO2 ) is specified. For PCC in power plants, the target CO2 removal rate is typically 90%. Two series of experiments are carried out: Series A with the CO2 partial pressure of 54 mbar in the flue gas, corresponding to gas-fired power plants and Series B with the CO2 partial pressure of 102 mbar in the flue gas, corresponding to coal-fired power plants (CESAR, 2011; Rolker and Arlt, 2006). For both series of experiments removal rate is 90%. The operating conditions are summarized in Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a direct setting of the CO2 removal rate is not possible it has to be adjusted by suitably choosing the reboiler duty in an iterative process during each experiment. The reboiler duty measured in the experiments must be corrected for heat losses. These have been analyzed in detail for the experiments with MEA (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011) using the method proposed by Notz (2010). As the temperatures and fluid dynamic conditions do not differ significantly it is reasonable to use the heat loss numbers found for MEA also for the other solvents. The heat losses are 1.5 kW for experiments in which no flashing occurs in the rich/lean heat exchanger and 1.3 kW otherwise. The flashing regularly occurred for the experiments at the high CO2 partial pressure due to limitations of the outlet pressure of pump P2. For a discussion of the flashing, see Oyenekan and Rochelle (2006). All numbers reported here for the heat duty are already corrected for these losses. The numbers for the heat losses are in good agreement with those reported by Notz (2010) for his experiments with MEA but with a different packing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results from the experiments with variation of the solvent flow rate allow plotting the regeneration energy versus the solvent flow rate or the ratio of the solvent flow rate to the flue gas flow rate, which is called L / G ratio here. From these plots an optimum solvent flow rate and the corresponding minimum regeneration energy for a given removal rate and CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas can be found. These optima are then compared for the different solvents. The comparison of the optima for the different solvents will give a reasonable ranking of the solvents provided that the plant is suitably designed for all studied solvents. This could, for e.g., not be the case if one of the solvents has much slower kinetics than the others and would, hence, require distinctly higher columns. For exploring that influence, a second set of experiments are carried out in which the flue gas flow rate is varied at a fixed L / G ratio.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For these series of experiments, the CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas is always 102 mbar and the removal rate is 90%. The flue gas flow rate ( F -factor) and solvent flow rate are varied simultaneously in a way so that the ratio of the flow rates ( L / G ratio) is kept constant. As also all other variables like the CO2 removal rate, and the temperatures are kept constant, this allows studying kinetic effects. Namely, upon decreasing the flow rates, also the amount of the CO2, which is transferred between the gas and the solvent decreases, both in the absorber and the desorber. Hence, less driving force for the mass transfer is needed. Therefore, mass transfer limitations will become less important, if they are present. Note that the available interfacial area is also affected by the decreased fluid dynamic load, which partially compensates the effect described above. texts.text: For these series of experiments, the CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas is always 102 mbar and the removal rate is 90%. The flue gas flow rate ( F -factor) and solvent flow rate are varied simultaneously in a way so that the ratio of the flow rates ( L / G ratio) is kept constant. As also all other variables like the CO2 removal rate, and the temperatures are kept constant, this allows studying kinetic effects. Namely, upon decreasing the flow rates, also the amount of the CO2, which is transferred between the gas and the solvent decreases, both in the absorber and the desorber. Hence, less driving force for the mass transfer is needed. Therefore, mass transfer limitations will become less important, if they are present. Note that the available interfacial area is also affected by the decreased fluid dynamic load, which partially compensates the effect described above. The influence of the fluid dynamic load on the effective interfacial area of Sulzer BX 500 has been studied

texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows the regeneration energy as a function of the ratio of the solvent and the flue gas mass flow ( L / G ratio) for the experiments of the Series A that were carried out at a CO2 partial pressure of 54 mbar and a removal rate of 90% (cf. Table 2). Three solvents are compared: MEA, CESAR1 and CESAR2. The data points shown for MEA in Fig. 3 are taken from Mangalapally and Hasse (2011). All other data were measured in the present work. Both CESAR1 and CESAR2 have advantages compared to MEA. Both the optimum L / G ratio and the regeneration energy at the optimum required for CESAR1 and CESAR2 are at lower numbers compared to MEA, which is in line with the expectations from the equilibrium data, cf. Section 3.1. CESAR1 seems to be the most promising.

texts.orig: Figs. 7 and 8 give the results for the absorber. The magnitude of the temperature bulge is increased by a greater enthalpy of absorption and by a greater change in gas phase CO2 concentration (Kvamsdal and Rochelle, 2008). For both CESAR1 and CESAR2 the absorber temperature profiles are similar, cf. Fig. 7. For high L / G ratios most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the lower part of the absorber column resulting in a larger temperature bulge. As a result, high liquid temperatures are observed in the absorber bottom for high L / G ratios, cf. Fig. 7. At low L / G ratios, the temperature bulge is smaller and from the slope of the experimental temperature profile it is expected that the temperature maximum is nearer to the top of the column, since most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the upper part of the absorber column. This is supported by the finding that higher gas temperatures are observed at the absorber top for low L / G ratios; cf. Fig. 7 (filled symbols). For both CESAR1 and CESAR2 the maximum temperatures achieved at different L / G ratios are similar and in the range 69-75 1 C. These results indicate that the enthalpy of absorption is similar for CESAR1 and CESAR2.

texts.orig: Fig. 8 shows the liquid phase mass fraction profiles of CO2 measured in the absorber as a function of the L / G ratio. The results are in line with the temperature profiles. As mentioned above for both CESAR1 and CESAR2, at high L / G ratios most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the lower part of the absorber (see the concentration gradient in Fig. 8), resulting in higher temperatures in the absorber bottom. Higher temperatures correspond to lower CO2 loadings. At low L / G ratios most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the upper part of the absorber (see the concentration gradient in Fig. 8). As a result lower temperatures are observed in the absorber bottom, corresponding to higher CO2 rich loadings.

texts.orig: The absorber operating lines are simply depicted by straight lines connecting the operating point at the absorber bottom and top. The linearized operating lines are plotted for the absorber, as the experimental data for the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase is available; at the inlet and outlet of the absorber. Note that this is not the case for the desorber. Together with the absorber operating line also the equilibrium curve at 40 1 C (absorber conditions) for different solvents is shown. Also results for MEA taken from Mangalapally and Hasse (2011) are included for a comparison. The distance between the operating line and the equilibrium curve gives an indication of the driving force for the mass transfer. The absorber operating line touches the equilibrium curve for MEA, cf. Fig. 11, indicating that vapor-liquid equilibrium is reached for MEA. CESAR1 and CESAR2 need a higher driving force than MEA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the L / G ratio was kept constant, cf. Section 3.2.2. As also all other parameters like the CO2 removal rate and feed temperatures were kept constant, this allows studying kinetic effects. Upon reducing the flue gas flow rate at constant removal rate, the CO2 mass flow in the flue gas is reduced and so is the CO2 mass flow, which has to be transferred between the gas and the liquid phase, both in the absorber and the desorber. As a consequence, a lower driving force is sufficient at lower flue gas flow rates and it is expected that the absorber operating line is shifted to higher loadings. Higher lean loadings result in an easier separation task in the desorber, so that a reduction of the stripping stream demand is expected (Mangalapally et al., 2009).

texts.orig: Fig. 14b gives more details. For the experiments carried out at L / G ¼ 2.5; the CO2 rich and lean loadings increase with decrease in F -factor, but the difference between the CO2 loadings of the rich and the lean solvent is constant, since the CO2 removal rate and the L / G ratio are kept constant. For all solvents this behavior is similar. The increase in the CO2 rich and lean loadings with decrease in F -factor is slightly higher for CESAR2 compared to the other solvents. This is in line with the fact that the decrease in regeneration energy is slightly higher for CESAR2 as compared to the other solvents. Note that for experiments carried out with CESAR2 at L / G ¼ 1.56; the CO2 rich and lean loadings remain almost constant with decrease in F -factor, cf. Fig. 14b. This is a consequence of the fact that the equilibrium CO2 loadings are approached.

texts.orig: For each solvent different series of experiments were carried out. For each of the series the partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas and the CO2 removal rate are kept constant, two different numbers for the partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas are specified in different series. CO2 removal rate was always 90%. In each series of experiments the solvent flow rate is varied and the optimal solvent flow rate leading to a minimum energy requirement is determined. The optima found in this way for different solvents are compared.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The absolute numbers for the optimal solvent flow rate and regeneration energy depend not only on the specified partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas and the CO2 removal rate but also on the design of the pilot plant. This is undesired as the goal of the study is a ranking of the solvents regarding future installations of which the design presently cannot be known. Two strategies are applied to solve this problem. Firstly, MEA is used as a reference solvent and not the absolute numbers for the optimal solvent flow rate and regeneration energy are used for the evaluation, but the relative change compared to MEA. It is not unreasonable to believe that relative change is distinctly less sensitive to the plant's design than the absolute numbers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Even such relative comparisons can lead to erroneous results if the studied solvents show large differences in their mass transfer kinetics and if the plant is not so amply sized that these kinetics do not play a role, which will rarely be the case. The influence of mass transfer kinetics on the results can be directly studied in the pilot plant. For that purpose, the flue gas flow rate is varied at a constant ratio of the solvent mass flow to the flue gas mass flow ( L / G ratio) and a constant removal rate and partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas. Low flue gas flow rates lead to a low mass flow of captured CO2 so that the amount of CO2, which has to be transferred per second through the interfacial area, decreases. The effect of lowering flue gas flow rate in the way described above is, hence, similar to that of increase in the column height at constant flue gas flow rate.

texts.orig: The results for the two studied solvents indicate similar influence of mass transfer kinetics on the results. Thus, the absolute numbers for the regeneration energy measured in the solvent flow variation study for both solvents compared to MEA are straightforward. Both CESAR1 and CESAR2 show improvements compared to MEA. The most promising is CESAR1 with a reduction of about 20% in the regeneration energy and 45% in the solvent flow rate.
